title: Funnel cake
subtitle: American fairground fried batter in a spiral lace pattern
excerpt: A classic American state fair food: batter poured through a funnel in a spiral or criss-cross pattern directly into hot oil, where it sets into a lacy, crisp, irregularly shaped disc in under 3 minutes. Immediately dusted with icing sugar. The batter is a simple mixing-bowl job; the technique of pouring through the funnel into the hot oil is the only skill involved, and it is learned in one attempt.
sourceNotes: Funnel cake is documented in American fairground and Pennsylvania Dutch culinary tradition from the nineteenth century. The Pennsylvania Dutch (German immigrant) community brought a similar fried batter tradition, described in American domestic and regional cookbooks from the late nineteenth century. Funnel cake in its current state fair format is described in American cookery guides from the early twentieth century, now in the public domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can be made up to 1 hour ahead. Stir before pouring. Funnel cakes must be eaten immediately; they go soggy within minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried in vegetable oil at 190°C. Higher than doughnut temperature: the batter is thin and cooks in under 3 minutes per side.

recipeTools.notes: Or wide frying pan; at least 28 cm diameter for a full-sized funnel cake.

glossaryTerms.definition: Controlling the flow of batter through a funnel held close to the surface of the hot oil, moving in a spiral from the centre outward to create an overlapping lacy disc. Cover the funnel spout with a finger to fill it, then remove the finger over the oil and move the funnel in a continuous spiral or figure-eight pattern. The batter strands should overlap and connect to form a cohesive disc rather than separate threads. A plain piping bag or a squeezy bottle with a small hole works just as well as a traditional funnel.

content.text: The batter is ready in under 5 minutes; the technique of
pouring the batter in a spiral into the oil
takes one attempt to get right. Use a wide pan so the cake has room to spread without touching the sides. Have the icing sugar ready: funnel cakes need to be dusted and eaten immediately.

Whisk together the flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt in a large bowl. In a separate bowl, beat the eggs with the milk and vanilla. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and whisk until the batter is smooth and pourable, about the consistency of double cream.

Heat the oil in a wide pan to 190°C. Hold a funnel or piping bag close to the oil surface, cover the opening, fill with about 120 ml of batter, then release over the oil in a continuous spiral pattern working from the centre outward, overlapping the strands to form a connected disc about 15 cm across. Fry for 90 seconds until golden on the underside. Flip carefully with tongs and fry for a further 60 to 90 seconds. Remove to a rack, dust generously with icing sugar, and serve immediately.
